segments.s368-g65: همی + past verb (گشت) is a classical/perhaps poetic imperfective: it marks a repeated or continuous past action — “kept turning.” بر پیش is a prepositional phrase (بر = toward/on, پیش = front/in front) giving direction. گردان چین is a nominal phrase where گردان (literally “revolving/turning”) qualifies چین (“fold/pleat”); word order places the modifier before the noun in this poetic line.
segments.s368-g66: بسان is a simile marker “like/as.” یکی here means “one/a certain” used idiomatically with the following noun. کوه بر پشت زین is a spatial simile: بر = on/upon, پشت = back, زین = saddle — literally “like one mountain upon the back of the saddle.”
segments.s368-g67: همانا is a strong discourse particle meaning “truly/indeed.” چنو (a variant of چون/چنان) = “such/like this.” نیز here functions as “also/indeed” reinforcing the statement. دیده is the noun “eye” (not the past participle here) and ندید is the negative past of دیدن (“did not see”) — together meaning the eye had seen nothing like it.
segments.s368-g68: ز is the poetic preposition “from/because of.” خوبی here means beauty/goodness (i.e., the remarkable appearance). کجا بود is a rhetorical question using بود (past of بودن) “was/were” to ask “where would/was ...?” چشمش = “his eye” (possessive -ش). رسید (past of رسیدن) means “reached”/“attained.” The line asks rhetorically: given such beauty, where could his sight possibly reach?
segments.s368-g69: یکی ترک = “a certain Turk” (یکی used as 'someone/a certain'). تیری is the object “an arrow.” برو here is part of a verbal cluster; in this poetic register it functions with برگماشت to indicate movement toward taking or seizing (here: “took up”); برگماشت (the compound) is used in the sense “took/pluck(ed) up” (literally ‘took upon’).
segments.s368-g70: ز = “from” (poetic). پشتش = پشت + -ش “his back.” سر تیر = “the head of the arrow” (سر = head, تیر = arrow). بیرون گذاشت is a simple past verb phrase: بیرون = out/outside, گذاشت = put/placed — together “put out” / “thrust out.” The whole means he thrust the arrow’s head out from behind his back.
segments.s368-g71: دریغ is an interjection/exclamatory noun meaning “alas” or “pity.” آن شه پروریده is a nominal phrase: آن = that, شه = king, پروریده = nurtured/reared — together “that king, reared (nurtured).” به ناز is an ablative/instrumental phrase “in/with pampering/indulgence” (ناز = pampering/affection). The phrase laments the pampered king.
segments.s368-g72: بشد here is the past/result sense “it happened/it became.” روی او = “before/at his face” (روی = face/front; او = he). باب = “gate/door” (metaphorical here), نادیده = “unseen/unknown” (literally ‘not-seen’), باز = “open.” Together: “the gate of the unseen opened before him,” a poetic way to say death or fate arrived.
segments.s368-g33: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' placed before its noun phrase (poetic inversion). یکی ناوکش is an indefinite noun phrase: یکی 'a certain' + ناوکش = ناوک + enclitic -ش (his), i.e. 'one of his arrows'. بر میان is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto the midst' (بر + میان) indicating direction/place and linking this motion to the following clause.
segments.s368-g34: گذارنده is an agentive/participial form ('placer' / 'that which passes/strikes') and شد is the past auxiliary 'became' (resultative), so گذارنده شد here means 'came to be lodged/acted as the striking thing'. بر سلیح کیان is a locative/genitive phrase: بر = 'on/upon', سلیح = 'weapon/armament', کیان = the royal house/the Kians (genitive), i.e. 'upon the Kayan weapon'.
segments.s368-g35: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بور here describes the steed/color ('the bay/fair one')—a common epic epithet for a horse; اندر افتاد is the compound verbal expression 'fell down / fell in' (اندر + افتاد). خسرو نگون places the proper name with a following epithet: خسرو 'Khosrow' + نگون 'ill‑fated/doomed', the epithet after the name.
segments.s368-g36: تن is 'body'. پاکش is the adjective پاک 'pure/clean' with enclitic -ش 'his' (i.e. 'his pure [body]'). آلوده شد is the resultative passive/verb phrase 'was made/ became soiled/was stained'. پر ز خون is a postposed qualifier: پر 'full (of)' + ز خون 'with/of blood', giving 'became full of blood'.
segments.s368-g37: دریغ is an interjection 'pity/alas' introducing regret. آن نکو روی is a noun phrase 'that good/fair face' (آن 'that' + نکو 'good/fair' + روی 'face'). همرنگ ماه is a simile/adjective phrase 'the same color as the moon' (همرنگ = 'same‑colored, like'; ماه = 'moon' used poetically for beauty) modifying 'نکو روی'.
segments.s368-g38: که is a relative 'that/which'. بازش is باز + enclitic -ش here meaning 'again' (باز) with the clitic referencing the person/object ('him/it'). ندید is the simple past negative 'did not see'. آن خردمند شاه is the agent/subject phrase 'that wise king' (آن = 'that/the', خردمند = 'wise', شاه = 'king'), placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s368-g39: بیامد is past 'came'. بر شاه is the prepositional phrase 'upon/against the king' (بر + شاه). شیر اورمزد can be read two ways in epic register: either 'a lion of Ohrmazd' (شیر as noun modified by اورمزد) or an exclamation invoking Ohrmazd; here it is taken as 'a lion (sent) of Ohrmazd' that came upon the king.
segments.s368-g40: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. The sequence ز + و + گرفتی should be read as the archaic/poetic contraction z(o) = 'from him/of him' (ز + او) plus گرفتی 'you took/seized' (2nd sg past). شهنشاه is vocative 'O king/emperor' (address). پزد is a terse epithet/verb here; one available sense is 'boast' (i.e. 'O boasting king') or related verbal use — the line therefore reads as an address: 'Where did you seize him, O boastful emperor?' (the exact nuance of پزد is archaic/poetic).
segments.s368-g49: بیامد is a past tense verb ('came') placed before its phrase (a V‑initial, poetically common order). یکی تیرش is an indefinite noun phrase 'one / a certain (of) his arrow(s)' with ش as the possessive suffix on تیر. اندر قفا is a prepositional locative phrase 'into the nape / at the back (of the neck),' where اندر = 'in/into' and قفا = 'nape/back of the neck.'
segments.s368-g50: شد is the past/resultative verb 'became / was' used here as the factual/perfective copula meaning 'came to be' or 'perished.' آن is the demonstrative/article 'that/the,' introducing the person named. خسرو شاهزاده is the name/title in apposition ('Khosrow, the prince'). فنا is a predicate nominal 'annihilation / ruin,' so the clause says literally 'that Khosrow the prince became annihilation' = 'that prince perished.'
segments.s368-g51: بیامد is again the past 'came.' پسش 'after him / following that' is an adverbial/locative referring to the previous event/person. باز here means 'again' (an adverb modifying آمد). شیدسپ شاه is the name and title 'Shidasp the king' (name followed by the title). The line therefore places Shidasp arriving immediately after the former event/person.
segments.s368-g52: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause describing شیدسپ. ماننده = 'resembling / like' and takes the following noun as its comparison. شاه here repeats the idea 'a king' (so: 'resembling a king'). بد is an archaic/poetic modifier; in context it does not convey simple 'evil' but functions as a short emphatic/qualifying word (seen in older Persian poetry) — the precise nuance is poetically flexible, so it intensifies or qualifies the comparison. همچو ماه is a simile 'like the moon.' Together the clause reads 'who, like a king, (in a poetically marked way) was like the moon.'
segments.s368-g53: یکی دیزه‌ای is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain fort / an outwork' with the suffix -ای adding indefiniteness/partitive. بر نشسته is a compound verbal phrase 'sat upon / was set on' (بر + past participle/staive نشسته). چو introduces the simile 'as / like' and نیل here is used in its color sense 'indigo / deep blue‑black' (a common poetic simile). The overall phrasing is V‑initial/nominal phrase followed by a simile describing appearance.
segments.s368-g54: This is a pair of parallel attributive phrases introduced by به marking manner/quality: به تگ همچو آهو = 'in narrowness/limb like a gazelle' and به تن همچو پیل = 'in body like an elephant.' تگ here means 'narrow / slender' (describing limb or frame), همچو = 'like,' آهو = 'gazelle,' تن = 'body,' and پیل = 'elephant.' The construction uses repeated به + attribute to list contrasting similes.
segments.s368-g55: به آوردگه گشت is 'he turned / went to the arrival-place/venue' (به + place noun + گشت, here گشت = 'went/turned'). و is the coordinating 'and.' نیزه بگاشت is an archaic perfective construction 'he set/placed the spear' (بگاشت = 'placed, fixed'); the object نیزه follows the coordination. The word order is typical epic/Persian: verb+locative then conjunction and verb+object.
segments.s368-g56: چو here means 'when / after' and introduces a temporal clause. لختی = 'a little while / shortly.' بگردید (from گشتن/گردیدن) means 'he turned / moved about' (here 'he turned/again changed posture'), and نیزه بداشت in this context functions as 'he bore/raised/took up the spear.' Note: بداشت is the past form used here to mean 'took up/held' (a poetic variant of برداشت); in printed lexica one may see variant lemmas but the intended meaning in context is 'lifted/held the spear.'
segments.s368-g17: بشد is the narrative/perfective verb meaning 'became' or 'happened' (here: 'vanished'); the verb precedes the noun in this elevated, somewhat archaic word order. آفتاب (the subject) plus the prepositional phrase از جهان ('from the world') give the source, and ناپدید is a predicative adjective/participle meaning 'vanished' — together: 'the sun became vanished from the world' = 'the sun disappeared from the world.'
segments.s368-g18: چه here is an exclamatory/interrogative particle 'what/how' used rhetorically; داند is present-tense 'knows' (classical narration uses present for general truths). کسی is the indefinite 'anyone'; کان is an archaic relative/connector equivalent to 'that/who' (classical 'که آن'), linking to شگفتی 'wonder' as its object. ندید is past negative 'did not see' (ن‑ + دید), so the clause reads roughly 'who can know (it) who has not seen that marvel.'
segments.s368-g19: بپوشیده شد is a passive/perfective construction: the past participle پوشیده with the copula/auxiliary شد gives 'was covered' or 'became covered.' چشمه آفتاب is a genitive noun phrase 'the fountain/spring of the sun' (source of sunlight) and is the patient of that passive.
segments.s368-g20: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from'; پیکانهاشان is پیکانها (arrows) + شان (their) = 'their arrows.' درفشان functions adjectivally/adverbially 'flashing / glittering' and چو آب is a simile 'like water' — literally 'from their arrows, flashing like water.'
segments.s368-g21: تو گفتی here is the 2nd-person past 'you said' (classical narrative uses گفتی). جهان is the subject 'the world'; ابر دارد is a possession-like construction using داشتن ('has'), so 'the world has clouds.' همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle (here marking the habitual/ongoing sense: 'always/kept').
segments.s368-g22: وزان = و + ز آن 'and from that' (linking to the previously mentioned cloud); ابر is 'cloud'; الماس is literal 'diamond' used metaphorically for bright drops; بارد is an archaic/poetic verbal form of باریدن ('to fall / to rain') meaning 'fell/was falling' (classical Persian often uses بارد for می‌بارد/بارید); همی again gives the continuous/habitual sense — 'diamonds were falling from that cloud.'
segments.s368-g23: وزان again 'and from that/from them' (linking to the forces); گرزداران is a plural agent noun 'mace-bearers' (گرز + دار + ان), و is 'and', and نیزه‌وران are 'spearmen' or 'lance-bearers' — a coordinated subject phrase naming the attackers.
segments.s368-g24: همی تاختند is the past plural verb 'they charged/attacked' with همی giving a continuous/emphatic tone. آن برین این بران is a classical pair of spatial adverbials: آن + بر + ‑ین ≈ 'on that side' and این + بر + ‑ان ≈ 'on this side' (the elements بر + the suffixes form adverbial locatives), so the phrase means 'they rushed/attacked there and here' (i.e. 'here and there').
segments.s368-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. اندر گذشت is a compact poetic locative + past verb: اندر 'in/within' + گذشت 'passed' (past), with آن شب 'that night' as the time phrase; word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb. و بود روز uses بود as a past copula 'was/wascome' with روز 'day'—literally 'and day was' (= 'and day came').
segments.s368-g2: بتابید is the simple-past of تابیدن 'to shine' (verb precedes subject, a frequent poetic order). خورشید is the subject 'the sun', and گیهان فروز is a postpositive epithet meaning 'world-illuminating' (گیهان 'world' + فروز 'illuminating/bright').
segments.s368-g3: به زین بر is a prepositional phrase indicating location 'onto/on the saddle' (به + زین with the additional locative بر). نشستند is a past plural 'they sat/took their seats' (verb before subject is allowed in poetic word order). هر دو سپاه 'both armies' is the plural subject (or topical phrase) referring to the two hosts.
segments.s368-g4: همی here is an archaic particle often marking continued or emphatic action ('still'/'continually'); دید is the past 'saw'/'looked'. زان کوه is a compact locative 'from that mountain' (زان = از + آن), and گشتاسپ شاه is the noun phrase 'Gushtasp the king' placed after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s368-g5: چو is the temporal 'when'. از کوه is the place phrase 'from the mountain' and precedes the verb; دید is the past 'saw'. آن شه بافرین is the noun phrase 'that praiseworthy king' (آن 'that' + شه 'king' + بافرین an epithet 'praiseworthy'), here placed after the verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s368-g6: کجا is the interrogative/adverb 'where' used rhetorically to introduce the subordinate clause. برنشستند is past plural 'they sat/upon' (mounted/took seats), گردان is the collective 'troop(s)', and به زین is the locative 'on the saddles' (به + زین). Word order (verb before subject/phrase) reflects poetic syntax.
segments.s368-g7: سیه رنگ is a compound epithet 'dark-colored' (سیه 'black/dark' + رنگ 'complexion/appearance') placed before the proper name بهزاد; را is the definite object marker marking Behzad as the one summoned. پیش here means 'before/in the presence of' and خواست is the past 'he summoned/willed' (poetic past used for 'called forward').
segments.s368-g8: تو گفتی is 'you said' (past addressing the addressee); که introduces reported speech. بیستونست is an older fused form of 'بیستون است' meaning 'it is Bisotun', and راست is a predicate adjective 'true' (i.e. 'it is truly Bisotun'). Note: the lemma senses provided for گفتی did not include the simple past 'you said', so that entry is flagged below.
segments.s368-g25: هوازی جهان بود شبگون شده is structured as Subject — Copula — Predicate. هوازی (literally “the air/atmosphere”) + جهان (“of the world”) forms the subject phrase; بود is the past copula (“was”); شبگون شده is a compound predicate where شده is the perfect/passive participle “has become” and شبگون is an adjectival description “night-colored/dusky.” Together they mean “the world’s air was become dusky.”
segments.s368-g26: زمین سربسر پاک گلگون شده places the locative noun زمین (“earth/ground”) before adverbial/intensifying modifiers. سربسر here intensifies or quantifies (’entirely/throughout’), پاک is an adjective “clean/pure,” and گلگون شده again uses شده as “has become” with گلگون “rosy.” Word order is noun + whole‑phrase modifier + predicate, yielding “the earth had everywhere become clean and rosy.”
segments.s368-g27: بیامد نخست آن سوار هژیر follows a typical Persian order: verb (بیامد, “came”) can appear first for narrative emphasis; نخست (“first”) and آن سوار هژیر (“that rider Hojir”) specify sequence and identification. The verb is past tense (came) and the phrase names the person who arrived first.
segments.s368-g28: پس شهریار جهان اردشیر uses پس (“then/next”) to mark sequence, followed by a title + genitival phrase: شهریار (“king”) + جهان (“of the world”) + اردشیر (proper name). The order is appositive: ‘then (came) Ardashir, king of the world.’ شهریار here functions as an epithet attached to the name.
segments.s368-g29: به آوردگه رفت نیزه به دست is a compact action clause: به آوردگه (“to the place of assembly/arrival”) is a prepositional goal phrase, رفت is the past verb “went,” and نیزه به دست is an instrument/possession phrase literally “spear in (his) hand” (= ‘with spear in hand’). Note the post‑verbal noun phrase functioning adverbially to indicate manner or instrument.
segments.s368-g30: تو گفتی مگر طوس اسپهبدست is a reported question/remark. تو گفتی is the 2nd‑person past (“you said”), مگر here functions as a rhetorical question marker (“isn’t it that / surely?”), and طوس اسپهبدست states the content: طوس (Tūs) + اسپهبد + است (contracted here as -ست) = “is a spahbed.” Note: the lexical senses provided for گفتی in the data do not include the 2nd‑person past ‘you said,’ so its sense index is marked -1 below.
segments.s368-g31: برین سان همی گشت پیش سپاه is an adverbial description of manner and motion. برین سان (“in this manner/so”) is a fixed demonstrative phrase (بر + این/ـین + سان), همی + گشت is a literary/imperfective past construction meaning ‘kept turning/kept going,’ and پیش سپاه means ‘in front of / before the army’ (پیش with مضاف‑الیه سپاه). The clause describes continuous movement toward/before the host.
segments.s368-g32: نبود آگه از بخش خورشید و ماه is a stative negative clause: نبود is the past negative copula ‘was not / there was not,’ آگه means ‘knowledge/awareness’ or ‘news,’ and از introduces the source/subject of that knowledge (بخش خورشید و ماه). Grammatically it says ‘there was no news/awareness about the (respective) portion/part of the sun and the moon’ — a poetic way of saying no report came concerning matters associated with the sun and moon.
segments.s368-g57: کدامست is an archaic fused question meaning “which is it?” (کدام + است). گفتا is the classical past-tense verb “he said,” often used parenthetically in epic narration. کهرم سترگ is an epithet in inverted order: کهرم (an older/ancient thing/person) + سترگ (eye / big‑eyed): here it functions as a descriptive phrase attached to the questioned person.
segments.s368-g58: کجا is the interrogative “where.” پیکرش is پیکر + enclitic ـش (“his/its body”), and the repetition پیکر پیر گرگ is appositive/emphatic: “the body — the body of the old wolf,” with پیر (old) modifying گرگ (wolf). Word order is noun + modifier (پیر گرگ) following the head (پیکر) for emphasis.
segments.s368-g59: بیامد is the simple past “came.” یکی before a noun is the indefinite/partitive use (“a certain / one”), here introducing the creature. دیو is “demon/fiend.” گفتا again is the past “said,” and منم is the emphatic present “I am” (the speaker identifying himself).
segments.s368-g60: که introduces a relative clause here (“who / who is such that”). با is instrumental “with” or “as,” linking the simile. گرسنه modifies شیر: “hungry (or ravenous) lion.” دندان زنم is a verbal phrase: دندان (tooth / fang) + زنم (1st‑person present/subjunctive of زدن ‘to strike’), here meaning “I strike/bite with (my) tooth/fang” — a vivid present used in reported speech.
segments.s368-g61: به نیزه بگشتند is a verbal sequence: به + نیزه marks the instrument/means (with the spear) and بگشتند is the past “they turned” (archaic b‑prefix). هر دو means “both.” چو باد is a simile “like the wind.” The order emphasizes the rapid, wind‑like motion.
segments.s368-g62: بزد is the simple past “he struck.” ترک را uses the object marker را after a definite noun (“the Turk” as direct object). نیزه شاهزاد is the agent/instrument phrase placed after the verb: “the prince’s spear” (literally “spear of the prince”) — Persian permits this postverbal placement for instruments/agents.
segments.s368-g63: ز here is the poetic preposition “from.” باره in this context means “the occasion / the blow/instance.” در آورد is a compound verb (در + آورد) meaning “to bring out / pull out” (در acts as a directional prefix ‘out’). و is “and.” ببرید (contextually from بریدن “to cut/sever”) takes سر “head” as its object: “and (he) cut off the head.”
segments.s368-g64: به خاک اندر افگنده is a verbal phrase: به خاک (to the earth) + اندر (in/into) + افگنده (past participle ‘cast/thrown’), so “cast down to the ground.” زرین کمر is an epithet: زرین (golden) modifying کمر (here ‘girdle / belt’ or by synecdoche ‘the girdled one’) — “the golden‑belted man.” The noun phrase follows the verb as the object/description of who was thrown.
segments.s368-g41: ز پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from ahead / from before'; اندر آمد is the past tense 'entered / came in' (verb + directional). به marks direction 'into / to', and دشت اندرا is the destination 'the plain (of) Andra'—in poetic prose the name follows the noun without an ezāfe marker.
segments.s368-g42: به زهر آب‌داده is a prepositional/adjectival phrase 'with/into poison water' where آب‌داده ('water-given', a compound participle) functions adjectivally as 'dipped/soaked (in water/poison)'. یکی خنجرا = 'a single dagger'; note the older poetic object form خنجرا (final -ا) and یکی as an indefinite/numercial modifier.
segments.s368-g43: خروشی برآورد is 'he uttered/raised a roar' (خروشی noun + برآورد, a poetic past/verb meaning 'brought forth/uttered'). شیر is the subject placed after the verb phrase (inverted word order typical in epic diction). برسان functions as a verbal complement meaning 'bringing/that reaches' tied to the utterance.
segments.s368-g44: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. آورد خواهد is an inverted future construction (standard order would be خواهد آورد = 'will bring'). ژیان is the object 'life', with گور زیر 'under the grave' as a locative phrase (noun + زیر 'under').
segments.s368-g45: ابر کین is an epithet 'a cloud of vengeance' (ابَر modified by کین); it precedes and qualifies the following noun phrase آن شاهزاده سوار ('that princely rider'). This fronted epithet is a poetic device to heighten the image before naming the warrior.
segments.s368-g46: بکشت is an archaic past-form (ب + کشت) meaning 'he killed/slain'. از introduces a partitive 'of/from' and marks the group killed: سواران دشمن 'the enemy's horsemen'. The numeral idea 'a thousand' (هزار) is given at the end of the line in classical ordering to indicate the quantity.
segments.s368-g47: به هنگامه is an adverbial phrase 'at the time/moment of'; بازگشتن is the verbal noun 'returning' and thus the head of the temporal clause, and ز جنگ 'from the battle' gives the source. The whole phrase means 'at the moment of returning from the war'.
segments.s368-g48: که here means 'when/which' introducing the descriptive clause. روی زمین 'the surface/face of the earth' is the noun phrase; گشته is the perfect/participial verb 'had become/turned'. بد لاله رنگ is an adjectival phrase 'bad / ill tulip-color' (بد qualifying the color likened to a tulip), so the earth's face 'had become' that sick/tawny tulip color.
segments.s368-g9: برو here functions as a temporal/discourse particle 'thereupon/then' (poetic usage), not an imperative. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' marking the target of the following verb. فگندند is an archaic past‑plural form (from افکندن) meaning 'they threw' or 'they cast'. برگستوان is an older/poetic form related to برگشتن and here acts as the second past verb 'they returned' (coordinated with فگندند: 'they threw ... and returned').
segments.s368-g10: برو again works as 'thereupon/then'. بر is the locative preposition 'on/upon' (introducing where the action takes place). نشست is the past 3rd‑singular of نشستن 'sat/down', functioning as the main verb. آن شه خسروان is a noun phrase 'that king (the king of kings)'—آن as demonstrative, شه (شاه) 'king', خسروان an honorific/plural epithet 'kings' used as a title ('king of kings').
segments.s368-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. هر دو = 'both/the two' (هر here intensifies the pair). برابر is adverbial 'face to face / opposite' describing their position. فرود آمدند is the compound past 'they came down / descended / alighted' (فرود آمدن used here as 'came down / drew up to meet').
segments.s368-g12: ابر پیل is a compound/metaphor: ابر 'cloud' modifies پیل 'elephant' to mean a host like a cloud of elephants. بر is the preposition 'upon/against'. نای 'ney' (reed‑flute) is the object; رویین 'iron' (postpositive adjective) qualifies نای. زدند is past plural 'they struck' and here can mean either physically struck or 'played/sounded' (in instrument contexts it often means 'to sound').
segments.s368-g13: یکی is an indefinite numeral/adjective 'one / a certain'. رزمگاهی (رزمگاه) is the object 'battlefield / place of combat'. بیاراستند is past‑plural 'they prepared/arranged/ornamented' (بیاراستن = to prepare/array). Word order places the object before the verb, common in poetic narrative.
segments.s368-g14: یلان is the plural subject 'the heroes/warriors'. هم ... هم (the first هم here) is part of a correlative/emphatic construction 'both ... and ... / likewise', with همی as an archaic particle adding emphasis. نبردان here is a verbal noun or plural noun 'battles / combat' serving as object. خواستند is past‑plural 'they desired/wished/sought'. Overall: 'The heroes too desired battle.'
segments.s368-g15: بکردند is past‑plural 'they did/made' (causative sense here: 'they caused'). یک تیرباران is the object 'a single shower/volley of arrows' (تیر + باران = arrow‑shower). نخست is the adverb 'first / at first', marking sequence (they first produced an arrow‑storm).
segments.s368-g16: بسان introduces a simile 'like / as'. تگرگ 'hail' and بهاران 'of spring' form the image 'hail of spring'. درست is an intensifier 'properly / exactly' (here: 'just like / exactly as'). The whole phrase compares the arrow‑shower to spring hail.

sentences.s368-s33: The front folds kept turning forward like a mountain on the saddle's back.
sentences.s368-s34: Indeed, the eye had never seen the like; of such beauty, where could his gaze reach?
sentences.s368-s35: A certain Turk took up an arrow and thrust the arrow's head out from behind him.
sentences.s368-s36: Alas for that king, reared in indulgence; the unseen gate opened before him.
sentences.s368-s17: One of his arrows came into the midst and lodged upon the Kayan weapon.
sentences.s368-s18: Khosrow, ill-fated, fell from the bay (steed); his pure body was soiled and became full of blood.
sentences.s368-s19: Alas for that fair, moon‑like face — the wise king did not see him again.
sentences.s368-s20: A lion of Ohrmazd came upon the king; where did you seize him, O boastful emperor?
sentences.s368-s25: One of his arrows came into the nape; that prince Khosrow perished.
sentences.s368-s26: After him, again came Shidasp the king, who, like a king, was as (bright) as the moon.
sentences.s368-s27: A certain fortress sat upon (the place), dark as indigo; slender in limb like a gazelle, in body like an elephant.
sentences.s368-s28: He turned to the place of arrival and fixed his spear; after a short while he turned and bore his spear (took it up).
sentences.s368-s9: The sun disappeared from the world; who can know it who has not seen such a wonder?
sentences.s368-s10: The sun's fountain was covered; from their arrows it flashed like water.
sentences.s368-s11: You said the world always has clouds, and from that cloud diamonds kept falling.
sentences.s368-s12: And from them the mace-bearers and spearmen likewise charged, attacking here and there.
sentences.s368-s1: When that night passed and day came, the sun that lights the world shone.
sentences.s368-s2: Both armies mounted/took their seats on the saddles, and King Gushtasp still watched from that mountain.
sentences.s368-s3: When the praiseworthy king saw from the mountain where the troop had taken their seats on the saddles.
sentences.s368-s4: He summoned the dark-complexioned Behzad forward; you said that it is Bisotun and true.
sentences.s368-s13: The world’s air had grown dusky; the earth, all over, had become clean and rosy.
sentences.s368-s14: First came that rider Hojir; then Ardashir, king of the world.
sentences.s368-s15: He went to the assembly-place with a spear in hand; you said, ‘Isn’t Tūs a spahbed?’
sentences.s368-s16: In this way he kept moving before the army; there was no news regarding the portion of the sun and the moon.
sentences.s368-s29: “Which is it?” he said — “the ancient, great‑eyed one; where is his body, the body of the old wolf?”
sentences.s368-s30: A demon came and said, “It is I who, like a hungry lion, strike with tooth/fang.”
sentences.s368-s31: Both turned their spears like the wind; the prince’s spear struck the Turk.
sentences.s368-s32: He drew (it) out from the blow and cut off the head, and cast the golden‑girt man down to the earth.
sentences.s368-s21: From ahead Andra entered the plain, bearing a single dagger dipped in poison.
sentences.s368-s22: The lion uttered a roar — a cry that would carry life under the grave.
sentences.s368-s23: That princely horseman, a cloud of vengeance, slew a thousand of the enemy's riders.
sentences.s368-s24: At the time of returning from the battle, when the surface of the earth had turned a sickly tulip-color.
sentences.s368-s5: Thereupon they cast (their weapons) away and returned; thereupon that king of kings sat down upon it.
sentences.s368-s6: When the two met face to face, a cloud of elephants struck (or sounded) their iron instruments upon the reed‑pipes.
sentences.s368-s7: They prepared a single battlefield; the heroes likewise wanted to engage in battle.
sentences.s368-s8: They made a first shower of arrows, exactly like the hail of spring.
